title: How to Move $200M in Client Assets in 21 Days: The Breakaway Advisor's Playbook

content:
A $200M breakaway advisor can complete their transition in 21 days — not 90 — if three conditions are met: a clean client list prepared before resignation, a technology platform at the receiving firm that eliminates manual repapering, and client communication that starts the moment the Broker Protocol window opens. The 90-day timeline is what happens when any of these three conditions is missing.

Key Takeaway: For a $200M advisor at 0.8% annual fees, each day of transition delay costs approximately $4,400 in revenue. A 70-day reduction — from 90 days to 21 — is worth $308,000 in captured revenue. That's the math behind choosing a firm with better transition technology.

Why Most Breakaway Transitions Take Three Times Longer Than They Should

The 90-day average isn't a law of physics. It's a consequence of three things that all have solutions.

Problem 1: The receiving firm runs repapering manually. New account forms are prepared one at a time, by hand. Each form takes 20–40 minutes. For a $200M advisor with 250 client accounts across three custodians, that's 750+ forms across a 3-custodian grid. At 30 minutes each, that's 375 hours of manual form preparation — before signatures and before submissions.

Problem 2: Forms hit custodians with errors. Without pre-submission validation, 60% of manually prepared forms are rejected on first submission (the industry NIGO rate). Resubmitting, waiting for the custodian to process, and resubmitting again adds weeks.

Problem 3: Client signatures get collected passively. PDF forms emailed to clients sit in inboxes for days. Clients who haven't signed don't get reminded until someone from the ops team follows up manually — which is three weeks after they should have.

Fix all three, and the 90-day transition becomes a 21-day transition.

What the 21-Day Breakaway Timeline Looks Like

The fastest breakaway transitions follow a specific sequence that overlaps steps wherever possible:

Every step after Day 3 runs in parallel, not sequentially. That's the operational design that makes 21 days achievable.

FastTrackr AI's end-to-end automation handles steps 2 through 5 automatically. Account mapping, form generation, pre-submission validation, digital signatures, and custodial submission all run without a coordinator manually preparing each step.

How to Prepare Before You Resign

The transition clock starts when you notify the receiving firm, not when clients sign. Every day of preparation you do before resignation is a day you don't lose after it.

Three to six months before:

  • Review your employment agreement for non-solicit, non-compete, and data ownership clauses.

  • Confirm whether your current firm is a Broker Protocol member. If yes, you can take client name, address, phone, email, and account title when you leave.

  • Choose your receiving firm and custodians. The receiving firm's transition technology is a major variable — ask specifically what their average transition timeline looks like.

30 days before:

  • Consult with a Broker Protocol attorney. Legal review for a straightforward breakaway runs $5K–$15K. Skip this step and risk $50K+ in litigation.

  • Document your client list in a format compatible with Broker Protocol rules.

  • Brief your most trusted clients informally (where legally permissible) on what's coming.

Day of resignation:

  • Resign and notify simultaneously with the receiving firm's client communication protocol.

  • Send client letters as soon as legally permissible.

  • Don't wait — every hour matters in the first 48 hours.

What to Look for in a Receiving Firm's Transition Technology

This question is underasked in breakaway advisor conversations. Most advisors negotiate hard on the transition deal (signing bonus, training support, payout) and accept whatever transition process the firm offers. That's a mistake.

The transition process is a revenue variable. Cerulli Associates data shows advisors lose an average of 18% of AUM during transitions. That variance — some advisors lose 10%, others lose 30% — is heavily influenced by how fast and how cleanly the transition executes.

Ask these questions before signing:

  • What is your average transition timeline for an advisor of my size?

  • What technology do you use for repapering? Is it automated or manual?

  • What is your NIGO rejection rate on first submission?

  • Do you support parallel multi-custodian processing, or do you handle custodians sequentially?

  • What does the client signature process look like? PDF email or digital portal?

A firm using FastTrackr AI's end-to-end automation can answer all of these specifically: 3-week average, AI-automated forms, 95% NIGO reduction, parallel multi-custodian, digital client portal. A firm running manual workflows will be vague on all of them.

How Client Communication Affects AUM Retention

The advisor's relationship is the asset. The paperwork is just mechanics. But the paperwork has to be fast enough that clients don't have time to question the relationship during the gap.

The window between resignation and accounts fully transferred is when clients are most vulnerable to competitor outreach. Your prior firm knows you left. They will call your clients. Every day of transition delay is another day your clients are receiving competitor calls while their accounts are still pending.

The communication that retains clients isn't one letter on day one. It's:

  1. Day 1: Clear announcement of the move, why you chose the new firm, what happens next.

  2. Days 2–5: Signed forms back from clients, confirming they're moving with you.

  3. Day 10: Update — accounts are in process, everything is on track.

  4. Day 21: Confirmation — accounts are live at the new firm, here's your first statement.

That arc of communication, timed to a 21-day transition, is what 95%+ client retention looks like in practice. The same communication arc across a 90-day transition loses clients between steps 2 and 3 — when "in process" has been the status for six weeks.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a breakaway advisor transition typically take?

The industry average for a breakaway advisor transition is 60–90 days. Advisors with large books (300+ clients, multiple custodians) may experience 4–6 month timelines using manual processes. With purpose-built transition automation, advisors can complete the same transition in 3–4 weeks. The timeline is primarily determined by the receiving firm's technology infrastructure, not the advisor's book size.

What is the Broker Protocol and how does it affect a breakaway transition?

The Broker Protocol is an industry agreement that allows advisors moving between member firms to take client name, address, phone number, email, and account title without violating non-solicit agreements. If both your current and receiving firm are Protocol members, your transition has significantly lower legal risk and cost. Verify Protocol membership status before resigning — it fundamentally changes what you can do on day one.

How much AUM do breakaway advisors typically lose during transitions?

Per Cerulli Associates research, advisors lose an average of 18% of AUM during transitions. The range runs from approximately 10% for fast, well-organized transitions to 25–30% for slow, operationally chaotic ones. For a $200M advisor, that 8-percentage-point difference between best and worst case is $16M in assets. Transition speed is the primary variable that drives this range.

What should a breakaway advisor ask about a firm's transition technology?

Ask for specific numbers: average transition timeline, first-submission NIGO rejection rate, whether they use parallel or sequential custodian processing, and whether clients sign digitally or via PDF email. Vague answers indicate manual workflows. Specific answers — 3-week average, 95% NIGO reduction, parallel processing, digital portal — indicate automation infrastructure that will protect your AUM during the move.

How do I protect client relationships during a breakaway transition?

Three things protect client relationships during a breakaway: (1) Fast communication — tell clients what's happening on day one, not day three, (2) Fast execution — clients who see their accounts transferred in 3 weeks don't have time to second-guess, (3) No friction — accounts should transfer cleanly, with no errors the client has to notice or resolve. FastTrackr AI's automation addresses the second and third; the advisor handles the first.

What legal steps should a breakaway advisor take before resigning?

Before resigning: (1) Retain a Broker Protocol attorney to review your employment agreement ($5K–$15K for a standard review), (2) Confirm your current firm's Protocol status, (3) Document your client list in accordance with Protocol rules, (4) Review any non-compete or non-solicit clauses for scope and enforceability. Skipping legal review to save money is the most common expensive mistake in breakaway transitions.

How does transition speed affect a breakaway advisor's revenue?

For a $200M advisor at 0.8% annual advisory fees, each day of transition delay represents approximately $4,400 in revenue at risk from potential client attrition. Reducing the transition from 90 days to 21 days saves 69 days × $4,400 = approximately $303,600 in protected revenue — not counting the compounding effect of retaining those clients long-term. The receiving firm's transition technology is a direct revenue variable.
